g.text: 4. EACH SERVICE CHOOSES ITS OWN PERSISTENCE
5. REPORTING / ANALYTICS NEED A DIFFERENT STRATEGY
6. SCHEMA EVOLUTION IS THE TEAM'S OWN PROBLEM (UPSIDE)

text.tspan: Can't 'SELECT * FROM orders JOIN customers' if they're separate DBs.
SOLUTIONS:
u2022 API COMPOSITION: app fetches from each, joins in memory
u2022 CQRS: maintain a denormalized read-model fed by events
u2022 Data lake/warehouse: nightly ETL into one query store
Can't atomically 'CREATE order AND DECREMENT inventory' across DBs.
SOLUTIONS:
u2022 SAGA pattern (orchestration or choreography)
u2022 Outbox pattern for reliable events
u2022 Compensating transactions (undo what went wrong)
u2022 Accept eventual consistency
Customer's name may live in Customers DB and be CACHED in Orders DB.
This is FINE u2014 just keep in sync via events.
A tiny bit of staleness is the price of independence.
NORMALIZATION is no longer the goal; LOOSE COUPLING is.
POLYGLOT PERSISTENCE:
u2022 Orders: PostgreSQL (transactional, relational)
u2022 Catalog: Elasticsearch (search-optimized)
u2022 Sessions: Redis (TTL, key-value)
u2022 Analytics: Snowflake (columnar, analytical)
u2022 Recommendations: Neo4j (graph)
Each team picks the right tool for its job.
BI dashboards can't query 50 different DBs in 50 different formats.
SOLUTIONS:
u2022 CDC u2192 data lake (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks)
u2022 Event stream as source of truth for analytics
u2022 Materialized read models per use case
The operational DBs stay focused; analytical workload goes elsewhere.
Want to add a column? Want to rename? Want to switch from Postgres to MongoDB?
Nobody else needs to coordinate. THIS IS THE WHOLE POINT.
The service's API is its contract u2014 the schema is private.
